coredata.dc:description: Efficient and clean utilization of coal resources is critical to achieving carbon emission reduction and sustainable energy development. In this study, a series of amphiphilic nitrogen-doped coal-based carbon dots (C12 ∼ C18-NCDs) were synthesized from bituminous coal through a green oxidation-depolymerization process mediated by formic acid and hydrogen peroxide, followed by hydrothermal nitrogen doping, alkylation, and sulfonation. The resulting NCDs exhibited tunable amphiphilicity, as confirmed by comprehensive structural and interfacial characterizations. Increasing the alkyl chain length from C12 to C18 significantly reduced the critical micelle concentration (from 802 to 121 mg/L at 25 °C) and lowered the surface tension at CMC (from 28.13 to 25.31 mN/m). Notably, C18-NCDs achieved ultra-low oil-water interfacial tension (0.00119 mN/m) under 10 wt% NaCl and exhibited excellent wettability on hydrophobic paraffin surfaces (contact angle: 44.63°). Furthermore, the amphiphilic NCDs demonstrated outstanding dispersibility for multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), effectively suppressing aggregation due to the synergistic effects of π–π stacking interactions, hydrophobic anchoring from alkyl chains, and electrostatic repulsion induced by sulfonate groups. This work presents a scalable and sustainable approach for converting coal into advanced carbon nanomaterials with multifunctional properties, showing promising potential for applications in petroleum interfacial engineering, fuel emulsification, and nanomaterial dispersion.

head.ce:title: Amphiphilic nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from bituminous coal: Enhanced oil-water interfacial activity and nanomaterial dispersibility

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry & Environmental Engineering, Yangtze University, Jingzhou 434023, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry & Environmental Engineering, Yangtze University, Jingzhou 434023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Centers for Clean Production and Pollution Control of Oil and Gas Fields

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Engineering Research Centers for Clean Production and Pollution Control of Oil and Gas Fields, Jingzhou 434023, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Efficient and clean utilization of coal resources is critical to achieving carbon emission reduction and sustainable energy development. In this study, a series of amphiphilic nitrogen-doped coal-based carbon dots (C12 ∼ C18-NCDs) were synthesized from bituminous coal through a green oxidation-depolymerization process mediated by formic acid and hydrogen peroxide, followed by hydrothermal nitrogen doping, alkylation, and sulfonation. The resulting NCDs exhibited tunable amphiphilicity, as confirmed by comprehensive structural and interfacial characterizations. Increasing the alkyl chain length from C12 to C18 significantly reduced the critical micelle concentration (from 802 to 121 mg/L at 25 °C) and lowered the surface tension at CMC (from 28.13 to 25.31 mN/m). Notably, C18-NCDs achieved ultra-low oil-water interfacial tension (0.00119 mN/m) under 10 wt% NaCl and exhibited excellent wettability on hydrophobic paraffin surfaces (contact angle: 44.63°). Furthermore, the amphiphilic NCDs demonstrated outstanding dispersibility for multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), effectively suppressing aggregation due to the synergistic effects of π–π stacking interactions, hydrophobic anchoring from alkyl chains, and electrostatic repulsion induced by sulfonate groups. This work presents a scalable and sustainable approach for converting coal into advanced carbon nanomaterials with multifunctional properties, showing promising potential for applications in petroleum interfacial engineering, fuel emulsification, and nanomaterial dispersion.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As an abundant and economically viable fossil energy source, coal has long held a significant position in the global energy landscape [1,2]. However, conventional coal combustion is associated with substantial emissions of carbon dioxide, particulates, and hazardous gases, making coal utilization a major contributor to global climate change and environmental pollution [3,4]. Consequently, developing efficient and sustainable coal conversion technologies has become a pressing research priority. Achieving the high-value and clean utilization of coal resources is crucial for advancing carbon reduction goals and accelerating the transition to a more sustainable energy framework [5,6].

In recent years, the non-combustion, high-value conversion of coal resources has gained increasing attention, particularly in the synthesis of advanced carbon nanomaterials such as graphene, carbon nanotubes, and carbon dots (CDs). This approach is recognized as a promising strategy for achieving the clean and high-value utilization of coal resources [7,8]. Among these materials, CDs have garnered significant attention due to their outstanding photoluminescence properties, chemical stability, low biotoxicity, and versatile surface functionalization capabilities, enabling their potential applications in environmental protection, energy storage, biomedicine, catalysis, and nanocomposite materials [9,10]. Coal's abundant three-dimensional aromatic framework (sp2-hybridized carbon) and naturally occurring oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g., CO, COOH) make it an ideal precursor for the preparation of high-performance CDs [11]. Compared to traditional carbon sources such as graphite and carbon nanotubes, coal's unique three-dimensional amorphous structure enables efficient depolymerization and functionalization under mild oxidation conditions while avoiding the environmental risks associated with conventional corrosive acid oxidation processes [12]. This characteristic provides a solid foundation for the development of low-cost, high-performance functional CDs materials, which has drawn increasing academic interest in recent years [13]. Studies have demonstrated that coal-derived CDs exhibit promising applications in various fields such as sensing [14], photocatalysis [15], energy storage [16], heavy metal detection [17], and bioimaging [18]. Further research indicates that heteroatom doping (e.g., N, S, P) can significantly improve the surface functional properties of coal-based CDs, thereby enhancing their fluorescence emission efficiency, catalytic activity, and surface reactivity [19,20]. Additionally, some studies have attempted to introduce single hydrophobic surface modifications to coal-based CDs to improve their dispersibility in nonpolar media, enabling applications in oil-phase environments [21]. However, these existing functionalization strategies are generally limited to either hydrophilic or hydrophobic modifications alone. To date, the development of finely tuned amphiphilic modifications that simultaneously impart both hydrophilic and hydrophobic properties remains unexplored. This limitation imposes significant constraints on the performance regulation of coal-based CDs in complex interfacial environments, such as oil-water interfaces and nanoparticle dispersion systems.

Interfacial phenomena play a critical role in fuel processing, oil recovery, and energy-related material systems, where the amphiphilicity of additives is essential for regulating wettability and dispersion behavior at oil-water interfaces [22–24]. Although conventional surfactants are widely employed, their performance often deteriorates under harsh conditions such as high salinity, elevated temperatures, and complex oil phases, limiting their effectiveness in demanding industrial scenarios [25–27]. Recently, CD-based nanosurfactants have attracted growing attention as next-generation interface regulators due to their high surface area, tunable chemistry, environmental compatibility, and structural stability. These materials have shown promise in petroleum-related applications such as crude oil emulsification, formation protection, and nanofluid stabilization [28]. However, coal-based amphiphilic CDs with robust interfacial performance remain largely unexplored, which constrains the potential of coal as a low-cost, carbon-rich resource for advanced fuel-compatible nanomaterials.

To address this gap, this study presents the first report of a novel strategy for converting bituminous coal into amphiphilic nitrogen-doped carbon dots (C12 ∼ C18-NCDs). The coal-based CDs were initially synthesized via a green and mild oxidation-depolymerization process mediated by formic acid (HCOOH) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), followed by precise amphiphilic modification through hydrothermal nitrogen doping, alkyl chain grafting, and sulfonate functionalization. A comprehensive suite of characterization techniques was employed to analyze the elemental composition, chemical structure, fluorescence properties, and morphological features of the synthesized amphiphilic NCDs. Their surface and oil-water interfacial behaviors were systematically evaluated, including surface tension, critical micelle concentration (CMC), interfacial tension, wettability on hydrophobic surfaces, and dispersibility for multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs). These assessments offered valuable insights into the structure-property relationships governing the performance of the materials. This work not only introduces a scalable and sustainable route for the high-value utilization of coal resources, which supports clean energy development and carbon emission reduction, but also pioneers a tailored strategy for enhancing the interfacial activity of coal-based nanomaterials. The findings establish a solid theoretical foundation for the development of functional coal-derived materials with broad application prospects in oil and gas recovery, emulsified fuel systems, and nanomaterial dispersion stabilization.

Bituminous coal was obtained from Taiyuan, Shanxi Province, China. The coal contained 0.60 % moisture, 10.14 % ash, 13.00 % volatile matter, and 76.26 % fixed carbon, with elemental composition of 91.11 % C, 3.98 % H, 1.49 % N, 0.37 % S, and 3.05 % O. All chemicals were of analytical reagent (AR) grade and used without further purification. HCOOH (98.0 %), urea (99.0 %), diethylenetriamine (99.0 %), H2O2 (30 % solution), 1-chlorododecane (98.0 %), 1-chlorotetradecane (98.0 %), 1-chlorohexadecane (97.0 %), 1-chlorooctadecane (98.0 %), ethanol (99.8 %), methanol (99.8 %), sulfamic acid (99.5 %), sodium hydroxide (98.0 %), n-hexane (98.0 %), and kerosene were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Multi-Walled Carbon Nanotubes (MWCNTs) with a purity of 95 %, diameter of 5–15 nm, and length of 10–30 μm were supplied by Jiangsu Xianfeng Nano Materials Technology Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.15 MΩ was produced in-house using a laboratory-grade water purification system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of coal-based CDs and their derivatives
ce:section.ce:para: Unprocessed bituminous coal was first pulverized and sieved through a 200-mesh screen. Then, 200 mg of the coal powder was dispersed in 30 mL of a HCOOH/ H2O2 mixture (volume ratio 5:1). The mixture was sonicated for 30 min to ensure thorough dispersion of the coal particles. The resulting suspension was then transferred to a magnetic stirrer and stirred at 600 rpm for 18 h at room temperature. After the reaction, the suspension was centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 15 min to remove unreacted coal residues. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane to obtain the product solution. The solvent was subsequently removed via rotary evaporation, yielding the coal-based CDs.

The obtained coal-based CDs were mixed with diethylenetriamine (DETA) at a mass ratio of 1:10, using deionized water as the solvent. The mixture was then sonicated to ensure uniform dispersion before being transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The hydrothermal reaction was conducted at 200 °C for 12 h. Following the reaction, the resulting solution was dialyzed using a 1000 Da molecular weight cutoff dialysis bag for 24 h to remove unreacted residues and by-products. The purified solution was concentrated via rotary evaporation to remove the remaining solvent, yielding the nitrogen-doped coal-based CDs (N-CDs).

The synthesized N-CDs were dissolved in 30 mL of anhydrous ethanol, followed by the addition of 1-chlorododecane in a molar ratio equivalent to that of the diethylenetriamine. The mixture was stirred at 80 °C for 5 h. Upon completion of the reaction, the mixture was transferred to a 250 mL separatory funnel, and 20 mL of distilled water was added. After vigorous shaking, the mixture was allowed to stand for phase separation, and the lower organic phase was collected. To enhance purity, 5 mL of n-hexane was added to the collected solution, and the extraction process was repeated 2–3 times. The purified solution was then concentrated via rotary evaporation at 60 °C under reduced pressure to remove residual solvents and moisture. The resulting intermediate product was transferred to a round-bottom flask, dissolved in 30 mL of methanol, and heated to 80 °C. 50 mg of sulfamic acid was then added, while urea (4 % of the total mass of the reactants) was introduced as a catalyst. The mixture was heated to 90 °C and stirred for 6 h. After the reaction, the solution was cooled naturally to room temperature. An equimolar amount of sodium hydroxide was added for neutralization. The mixture was then dialyzed in a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 12 h to remove impurities. Finally, the purified product was vacuum-dried to obtain the target product, C12-NCDs. By replacing 1-chlorododecane with 1-chlorotetradecane, 1-chlorohexadecane, or 1-chlorooctadecane in the above procedure, the corresponding amphiphilic NCDs (C14-NCDs, C16-NCDs, and C18-NCDs) were synthesized following the same procedure. A schematic illustration of the synthesis process is presented in Fig. 1 .

The yield of coal-based CDs was assessed by fixing the coal powder mass at 200 mg, the H2O2 volume at 5 mL, and the reaction time at 12 h, while varying the volume ratio of HCOOH to H2O2 (1:1, 2.5:1, 5:1, 7.5:1, and 10:1). The yield was calculated as the mass of obtained CDs relative to the initial coal powder mass, expressed as a percentage. The volume ratio that produced the highest yield was then selected as the fixed condition to further examine the influence of reaction time (6, 12, 18, and 24 h) on the coal-based CDs yield. All experiments were performed in triplicate under identical conditions, and the average values were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of coal-based CDs and their derivatives
ce:section.ce:para: To systematically characterize the structure, surface functional groups, morphology, and optical properties of coal-based CDs, N-CDs, and a series of amphiphilic NCDs (C12-NCDs, C14-NCDs, C16-NCDs, and C18-NCDs), multiple analytical techniques were employed, including Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectroscopy, and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. For FT-IR analysis, the dried CD samples were thoroughly mixed with dried potassium bromide (KBr) at a mass ratio of 1:200, pressed into pellets, and scanned over the range of 4000–500 cm−1 using a Thermo Fisher Scientific NICOLET 6700 FT-IR spectrometer (USA) to identify surface functional groups. Elemental composition and chemical states were analyzed via XPS using a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (USA) equipped with an Al-Kα radiation source (hv = 1486.6 eV). The instrument was operated at a voltage of 12.5 kV with a filament current of 16 mA. The C1s peak at 284.8 eV served as the binding energy reference. Morphological characteristics were examined using a JEM-2100 TEM (JEOL, Japan) operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The CD samples were first dispersed in water at a concentration of 100 mg/L by ultrasonication for 10 min. A few drops of the resulting suspension were then deposited onto a copper grid coated with an ultrathin carbon film, followed by air drying before TEM imaging. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded using a TU-1900 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Persee General Instrument Co., China) over the range of 190–800 nm. The amphipathic NCD solutions were prepared at a concentration of 100 mg/L, with ultrapure water serving as the blank reference. PL properties were characterized using a Hitachi F-4000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) under the following conditions: excitation slit width of 5 nm, emission slit width of 10 nm, and scanning speed of 1200 nm/min. This analysis provided a systematic evaluation of the emission behavior of different amphipathic NCDs under varying excitation wavelengths.

A 1000 mg/L stock solution of C12-NCDs, C14-NCDs, C16-NCDs, and C18-NCDs was prepared using distilled water and subsequently diluted stepwise to obtain test solutions with concentrations ranging from 10 to 1000 mg/L. Surface tension was measured using a QBZY fully automatic surface tensiometer (Shanghai Fangrui Instrument Co., China) at 25, 35, 45, and 55 °C. The relationship between surface tension (γ) and concentration (C) was plotted to construct γ-C curves, from which the CMC was determined by identifying the intersection point of the linear fitting curves. Each experiment was conducted three times under identical conditions, and the average results were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size distribution and Zeta potential measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The particle size distribution and Zeta potential of C12-NCDs, C14-NCDs, C16-NCDs, and C18-NCDs solutions, prepared at concentrations exceeding their CMC, were analyzed at varying temperatures (25, 35, 45, and 55 °C) using a Zetasizer Nano particle size analyzer (Malvern Panalytical, UK). All experiments were repeated three times under consistent conditions, with average values reported.

The interfacial tension between kerosene droplets and amphiphilic NCD solutions (1000 mg/L) was determined using the rotating drop method with a TX-500C interfacial tensiometer (CNG Enterprises Ltd., USA). Measurements were conducted at 60 °C with a rotation speed of 5000 r/min, while varying the NaCl concentration at 0, 1, 3, 5, 7, and 10 wt%. Interfacial tension values were recorded at 5-min intervals until equilibrium was reached. Experiments were performed in triplicate under the same conditions, and mean values were recorded.

Glass slides were sequentially cleaned with distilled water and anhydrous ethanol (2–3 times) and then dried in an oven at 60 °C for 1 h. Paraffin wax was melted in a 100 mL beaker using an electric heater, and the cleaned slides were immersed in the molten wax for 10 s, then removed and air-dried to ensure a uniform wax coating. The contact angles of amphiphilic NCD solutions (10, 50, 100, 500, and 1000 mg/L) on paraffin-coated glass slides were measured at room temperature using a JC2000C contact angle goniometer (Shanghai Zhongchen Digital Technology Equipment Co., China). The measurement software was set to capture images at 10-s intervals to monitor the droplet morphology over time, allowing for the evaluation of the wetting behavior of amphiphilic NCDs on the paraffin-coated surface. Each test was carried out three times under identical conditions, and the average measurements were documented.

To assess the dispersibility of amphiphilic NCDs, 1 mg of MWCNTs was added to 5 mL of C12-NCD, C14-NCD, C16-NCD, and C18-NCD solutions at concentrations of 0, 1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 500, and 1000 mg/L. The mixtures were subjected to ultrasonication at 40 kHz for 30 min, followed by 3 h of static settling. Subsequently, 0.1 mL of the MWCNTs suspension containing 1000 mg/L amphiphilic NCDs was diluted 100-fold, followed by analysis of its particle size distribution and Zeta potential. After 72 h of static settling, the dispersion state of all samples was photographed and recorded. Each experiment was conducted in triplicate under controlled conditions, and the mean values were recorded.

Fig. 2 illustrates the variation in the yield of coal-based CDs as a function of the HCOOH/H2O2 volume ratio (Fig. 2a) and reaction time (Fig. 2b). When the reaction time was fixed at 12 h, increasing the proportion of HCOOH enhanced the yield from 13.3 % to 18.5 %, indicating that the acidic environment created by HCOOH facilitated the protonation of coal molecules, promoting their depolymerization and thereby enhancing the formation of coal-based CDs. The yield reached its maximum of 18.5 % at an HCOOH/H2O2 volume ratio of 5:1. However, further increasing the HCOOH ratio to 10:1 resulted in a yield decrease to 9.6 %, suggesting that excessive HCOOH intensified system acidity, accelerating H2O2 decomposition and generating excessive free radicals (e.g., ·OH and O2·). These radicals likely compromised the stability of the coal-based CDs, resulting in their degradation and subsequently reducing the yield. Regarding the effect of reaction time, with the HCOOH/H2O2 volume ratio fixed at 5:1, the yield of coal-based CDs gradually increased as the reaction time extended from 6 h to 18 h, reaching a maximum of 23.0 % at 18 h. This trend indicates that the depolymerization of coal molecules and surface functionalization reactions had sufficiently progressed, achieving optimal oxidation efficiency that promoted CD formation. However, extending the reaction time beyond 18 h led to a sharp decline in yield, suggesting that prolonged oxidation induced CD degradation and aggregation, thereby compromising product stability and reducing the overall yield. The synergistic effect between HCOOH and H2O2 played a pivotal role in coal-based CD synthesis. HCOOH facilitated the depolymerization of cross-linked coal structures by providing an acidic environment that promoted the release of aromatic core fragments. Meanwhile, HCOOH also regulated the pH, stabilizing the ·OH radicals generated from H2O2 decomposition, thus improving their effective utilization in coal oxidation reactions. Simultaneously, H2O2 decomposed to produce ·OH radicals, which selectively attacked aliphatic side chains and oxygen-containing bridge bonds within the coal structure, further releasing aromatic fragments. The oxidative action also functionalized the surface of the coal-based CDs, introducing carboxyl (–COOH) and hydroxyl (–OH) groups, thereby enhancing their hydrophilicity and stability [29].

Table S1 compares the experimental conditions, post-treatment steps, and yields of the HCOOH/H2O2 oxidation system with conventional acidic oxidation systems for CD synthesis. The results reveal that the HCOOH/H2O2 system achieved a relatively high yield of coal-based CDs under mild room temperature conditions, highlighting its green and efficient nature. Overall, the oxidation mechanism of the HCOOH/H2O2 system is governed not only by the ratio and synergistic effect of the two reagents but also by the dynamic regulation of reaction time. By optimizing the oxidant ratio and reaction duration, the stability and yield of coal-based CDs can be effectively controlled, providing valuable insights for their efficient synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural analysis of coal-based CDs and their derivatives
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of coal-based CDs, N-CDs, and amphiphilic NCDs (C12-NCDs, C14-NCDs, C16-NCDs, and C18-NCDs) was characterized using TEM. To determine the average particle size, 150 randomly selected particles were measured using Nano Measure software. As shown in Fig. 3 , all samples exhibited a well-defined graphite core with a consistent lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 3a1–f1), corresponding to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [30,31]. This uniformity confirms that nitrogen doping and alkyl sulfonation did not disrupt the sp2-conjugated carbon core structure of the coal-based CDs. Instead, surface functionalization primarily occurred at the CD edges or defect sites. Notably, the coal-based CDs (Fig. 3a1, a2) exhibited a relatively larger average particle size of 6.76 ± 2.00 nm with a broad size distribution ranging from 3.56 to 13.88 nm, indicating poor uniformity. This is likely attributed to incomplete oxidation of the coal matrix. Following nitrogen doping (N-CDs, Fig. 3b), the average particle size significantly decreased to 2.49 ± 0.39 nm. This reduction can be attributed to the intensified fragmentation of coal macromolecules in the hydrothermal environment. Additionally, the introduction of amino groups (–NH₂/–NH–) enhanced surface charge repulsion, effectively inhibiting particle aggregation. Subsequent alkylation resulted in a gradual increase in particle size (Fig. 3c2–f2), rising from 2.69 ± 0.46 nm for C12-NCDs to 3.58 ± 0.62 nm for C18-NCDs. This increase is likely due to the introduction of long-chain alkyl groups, which may induce partial aggregation through hydrophobic interactions. Moreover, the physical encapsulation of the CD core by the alkyl chains contributed to the observed size increase. The incorporation of sulfonate groups further enhanced the hydrophilicity of the alkylated CDs, improving their stability and dispersibility in aqueous solutions.

The FT-IR spectra of the synthesized CDs were recorded to investigate their surface functional groups and structural characteristics (Fig. 4a, b). In the FT-IR spectrum of coal-based CDs (Fig. 4a), a broad absorption band in the range of 3353–3061 cm−1 was attributed to OH stretching vibrations from carboxylic acid and phenolic hydroxyl groups [32]. A sharp peak at 1709 cm−1 corresponded to carboxylic CO stretching vibrations [33]. Peaks at 1641 cm−1 and 765 cm−1 were assigned to aromatic CC stretching vibrations and out-of-plane CH bending vibrations, respectively [17,34,35], confirming the presence of an sp2-hybridized carbon core. Following nitrogen doping via diethylenetriamine treatment, the FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 4a) displayed two new peaks at 1654 cm−1 and 1567 cm−1, corresponding to the amide I band (CO stretching) and amide II band (NH bending), respectively [36,37]. In addition, enhanced peaks in the range of 1376–1342 cm−1 were assigned to CN stretching vibrations [38], providing clear evidence that amino groups were covalently bonded to the CD surface via amide formation. Furthermore, an intensified absorption band between 1112 and 1037 cm−1 was attributed to C–O–C asymmetric stretching vibrations [36], suggesting that partial etherification cross-linking may have occurred during the hydrothermal process. This observation aligns with the TEM results, which showed a reduced particle size for N-CDs, further supporting the occurrence of structural modification.

Compared to N-CDs, the FT-IR spectra of amphiphilic NCDs (Fig. 4b) revealed three distinct sets of characteristic peaks, confirming their dual-functional structure comprising hydrophobic alkyl chains and sulfonate groups. Specifically, absorption peaks at 2956 cm−1, 2922 cm−1, 2852 cm−1, and 1460 cm−1 corresponded to asymmetric –CH₃ stretching, asymmetric –CH₂– stretching, symmetric –CH₂– stretching, and –CH₂– scissoring vibrations, respectively, indicating the successful grafting of long-chain alkyl groups [39]. Additionally, peaks at 1212 cm−1 and 1132 cm−1 were assigned to asymmetric and symmetric SO stretching vibrations, respectively, serving as fingerprint signals for –SO3 − groups [40]. The presence of a distinct peak at 1062 cm−1, attributed to SO or CO stretching vibrations, further confirmed the incorporation of –SO3 − groups on the CD surface. These FT-IR results provide compelling evidence of successful nitrogen doping and dual-functional modification.

The elemental composition and bonding states of the six synthesized CDs were analyzed using XPS, with the results presented in Fig. 4c, d, Fig. 5 , Table S2, and Table S3. The XPS survey spectrum of coal-based CDs (Fig. 4c and Table S2) indicated that their surface was predominantly composed of C (72.64 %) and O (24.22 %), with a minor amount of N (3.14 %), attributed to naturally occurring nitrogen-containing heterocyclic structures in the bituminous coal precursor. Following nitrogen doping via diethylenetriamine treatment, the nitrogen content in N-CDs increased significantly to 15.50 % (Table S2). The N1s spectrum (Fig. 5b2) revealed a new peak at 401.0 eV, corresponding to amide N [36], indicating that part of the diethylenetriamine was successfully grafted onto the CD surface via amide formation. Additionally, the contents of pyridinic N (399.00 eV), pyrrolic N (399.92 eV), and graphitic N (401.67 eV) increased accordingly (Table S3), suggesting that the introduced nitrogen participated in the reconstruction of the carbon core [41]. Nitrogen doping was further accompanied by a noticeable reduction in oxygen content, decreasing to 15.31 % (Table S2). In the C1s spectrum, the characteristic peak corresponding to O–C=O (288.90 eV) [42] diminished significantly or disappeared entirely, while a new peak at 288.06 eV emerged, corresponding to N–C=O [30]. This finding confirms the occurrence of amide formation, aligning well with the FT-IR analysis.

For the amphiphilic NCDs, the detection of S and Na elements (Fig. 4d), combined with the appearance of two characteristic peaks in the S2p spectrum (Fig. 5c4-f4) at 168–170 eV, corresponding to the S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 spin-orbit split peaks associated with –SO3 − groups [40,43], confirmed the successful incorporation of sulfonate groups on the CD surface. As the alkyl chain length increased from C12 to C18, the carbon content increased from 45.71 % to 60.91 % (Table S2), while the proportion of C–C/C=C bonds in the C1s spectrum rose from 54.39 % to 76.60 % (Fig. 5c1-f1 and Table S3), indicating the successful grafting of hydrophobic alkyl chains on the CD surface. These results align with the FT-IR data, further validating the successful dual-functionalization process. Further analysis of the O1s spectrum revealed a characteristic peak at 532.9 ± 0.2 eV (Fig. 5c3-f3), corresponding to SO, SO, or CO bonds [43]. The relative intensity of this peak was highest in C12-NCDs (32.81 %) and decreased to 14.83 % in C18-NCDs as the alkyl chain length increased (Table S3). This trend was consistent with the reduced intensity of the –SO3 − signal in the S2p spectrum (Fig. 5c4-f4 and Table S3). The gradual decrease in sulfur content (from 8.50 % to 6.69 %) is likely due to the shielding effect of the longer alkyl chains, which reduced the exposure of sulfonate groups within the XPS probing depth.

The UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 6a–d) revealed that all four amphiphilic NCDs exhibited a prominent absorption peak at 205–206 nm, attributed to the π → π⁎ transition of the sp2 carbon core [44]. This feature corresponds to the 0.21 nm graphite lattice spacing observed in the HRTEM images. The absorption intensity progressively weakened with increasing alkyl chain length, which is likely due to the hydrophobic encapsulation effect of the long-chain alkyl groups. This encapsulation may have weakened the delocalization of π-electrons within the carbon core, thereby reducing the overall absorption intensity. In addition, weak absorption bands were observed at 236–238 nm and 275–276 nm, corresponding to the n → π⁎ transitions of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing surface functional groups [45]. The intensities of these bands also decreased as the alkyl chain length increased. This trend correlates with the enhanced alkyl signals observed in the FT-IR spectra, suggesting that the introduction of long-chain alkyl groups effectively suppressed the exposure of surface defect states through steric hindrance, thereby reducing the number of active absorption centers.

Fluorescence spectroscopy provided further insights into the influence of surface chemical modifications on the photophysical behavior of the amphiphilic NCDs. Within the excitation wavelength range of 320–400 nm, all samples exhibited typical excitation-dependent fluorescence, characterized by a progressive redshift in the emission peak with increasing excitation wavelength. This behavior aligns with the emission characteristics governed by surface states in carbon dots [46,47]. Among the samples, C12-NCDs exhibited the most intense emission, with a peak spanning 412–459 nm (Fig. 6a), emitting a deep blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 6a inset). In contrast, the emission peaks of C14-NCDs and C16-NCDs were slightly redshifted to 416–454 nm and 428–457 nm, respectively (Fig. 6b, c). Their fluorescence intensities decreased accordingly, with the emission color gradually transitioned from blue to a slightly greenish hue (Fig. 6b, c inset). For C18-NCDs, the emission peak appeared at 426–460 nm (Fig. 6d), accompanied by the weakest fluorescence intensity and a cyan-colored emission (Fig. 6d inset). This color evolution from blue to cyan reflects the impact of alkyl chain length on the surface emission centers. NCDs with shorter alkyl chains retained more exposed polar groups or defect states, which facilitated strong blue emission. As the alkyl chain length increased, the shielding effect intensified, reducing the exposure of polar surface groups or defect states. Consequently, some radiative recombination centers were passivated, and molecular interactions were altered, resulting in diminished fluorescence intensity and a slight redshift in emission. Generally, N and O functional groups (e.g., CO, CN) significantly contribute to the fluorescence properties of CDs, where higher content of these groups typically enhance fluorescence intensity [41,48]. However, long-chain alkyl modification may reduce the exposure of localized defect states or introduce new hydrophobic interactions, thereby influencing emission efficiency and fluorescence color. The systematic comparison of UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra highlights the profound impact of alkyl chain length and surface functional group distribution on the optical properties of amphiphilic NCDs.

Surface tension is a crucial parameter for evaluating the surface activity of amphiphilic molecules. The extent of surface tension reduction and the CMC are indicative of a material's self-assembly performance and interfacial application potential. To explore the impact of alkyl chain length on the surface activity of C12 ∼ C18-NCDs, surface tension measurements were conducted across various temperatures (25–55 °C) as a function of amphiphilic NCD concentration, and the corresponding CMC values were determined. As shown in Fig. 7a–d, all samples exhibited typical micellization behavior, where surface tension decreased progressively with increasing concentration before reaching a plateau. This trend suggests that the amphiphilic NCDs exhibit distinct self-assembly behavior in aqueous solutions. The data presented in Table 1 reveal that the CMC values decreased significantly with increasing alkyl chain length. For instance, at 25 °C, the CMC of C12-NCDs was 802 mg/L, while that of C18-NCDs decreased to only 121 mg/L. This trend suggests that longer alkyl chains promote stronger hydrophobic interactions, enhancing self-assembly efficiency and facilitating micelle formation [49]. In addition, all samples demonstrated a decreasing CMC trend with rising temperature. For example, the CMC of C18-NCDs declined from 121 mg/L at 25 °C to 76 mg/L at 55 °C. This behavior is attributed to two competing effects. On one hand, increasing temperature disrupts the hydration shell surrounding the hydrophilic head groups, weakening hydration interactions and promoting micelle formation. On the other hand, elevated temperatures disturb the structured water network around the hydrophobic alkyl tails, reducing their tendency to self-aggregate and thereby hindering micelle formation [50]. The results of this study suggest that, within the C12-NCDs to C18-NCDs systems, the dominant factor was the reduction in hydration around the hydrophilic head groups, ultimately enhancing the micellization process. Further analysis of the surface tension at the CMC (γCMC) revealed that C18-NCDs exhibited the most effective surface activity, reducing the surface tension of water to 25.31 mN/m at 25 °C, which further decreased to 24.28 mN/m at 55 °C. Overall, increasing alkyl chain length and rising temperature jointly contributed to enhanced micellization behavior and improved surface activity of the amphiphilic NCDs, underscoring their potential for efficient interfacial regulation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size distribution and micelle stability
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the aggregation behavior of amphiphilic NCD micelles at concentrations exceeding their CMC, the particle size distribution and Zeta potential were analyzed using dynamic light scattering (DLS). As shown in Fig. 8a and Table S4, the average micelle size increased notably with the extension of the alkyl chain. The micelle size ranged from approximately 69–83 nm for C12-NCDs to 126–142 nm for C18-NCDs, suggesting that longer alkyl chains expanded the hydrophobic core volume and enhanced intermolecular hydrophobic interactions, resulting in the formation of larger aggregates [51]. In addition, all samples exhibited a decreasing micelle size trend with rising temperature. This reduction suggests that moderate heating improved the dynamic equilibrium between micelles, promoting a more compact micellar structure. The polydispersity index (PDI), a key parameter in DLS analysis, reflects the uniformity of particle size distribution and the consistency of micelle formation [52]. According to the data in Table S4, all samples exhibited PDI values below 0.4, indicating generally good size uniformity. Among them, C14-NCDs and C16-NCDs displayed relatively lower PDI values (minimum values of 0.124 and 0.142, respectively), suggesting the formation of more uniform micelles. Conversely, C12-NCDs showed a higher PDI (reaching up to 0.370), likely due to the structural variability induced by shorter alkyl chains, which increased micelle diversity. Meanwhile, C18-NCDs maintained stable PDI values in the range of 0.21–0.25, indicating that the longer hydrophobic chains contributed to enhanced micelle stability and structural uniformity [53]. It is important to note that the micelle sizes measured by DLS were considerably larger than the particle sizes observed via TEM. This discrepancy arises from the fundamental differences in measurement conditions: DLS measures the hydrated size of micelles or aggregates formed under CMC conditions, which includes not only the carbon dot core but also the surrounding hydration layer. In contrast, TEM captures the size of individual carbon dots in their dry state under conditions below the CMC threshold [54]. These methodological differences account for the observed variation in particle size measurements. As shown in Fig. 8b, all carbon dot micelles displayed highly negative Zeta potential values (absolute values exceeding 30 mV), indicating that the sulfonate groups imparted strong electrostatic repulsion, enhancing micelle stability in aqueous media. Notably, the absolute Zeta potential value increased with alkyl chain length, rising from −35.2 mV for C12-NCDs to −42.4 mV for C18-NCDs, suggesting that longer alkyl chains enhanced surface charge density, effectively suppressing further aggregation and sedimentation tendencies [50,55,56]. Conversely, as the temperature increased, the absolute Zeta potential values gradually decreased. This behavior may result from thermal agitation weakening both the hydrophobic interactions within the micelle core and the surface charge density, reducing the electrostatic repulsion between micelles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oil-water interfacial tension and salt-promoted adsorption effect
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 presents the effect of NaCl concentration (0–10 wt%) on the oil-water interfacial tension of nitrogen-doped coal-based carbon dots with varying alkyl chain lengths. The results demonstrate that all amphiphilic NCDs exhibited pronounced interfacial activity, as evidenced by a rapid decline in interfacial tension within the initial 20 min, followed by a gradual stabilization. This dynamic behavior reflects the rapid diffusion and adsorption of carbon dots from the aqueous phase to the oil-water interface, ultimately forming a stable interfacial film. The effect of alkyl chain length was particularly notable, with longer hydrophobic chains leading to a more substantial reduction in interfacial tension. Among these samples, C18-NCDs exhibited the most pronounced reduction in interfacial tension, achieving a minimum value of 0.02194 mN/m in the absence of NaCl, which was significantly lower than the 0.58866 mN/m observed for C12-NCDs. This superior performance can be attributed to the enhanced molecular packing of carbon dots with longer alkyl chains at the interface. The extended hydrophobic chains readily penetrate the oil phase, while the sulfonate groups remain stabilized in the aqueous phase, forming a denser and more stable interfacial film. Analysis of the electrolyte effect indicated that the introduction of 0–7 wt%NaCl significantly reduced interfacial tension in all carbon dot solutions, demonstrating a clear electrolyte-promoted interfacial adsorption effect [57]. This behavior can be attributed to the electrolyte-induced weakening of the hydration shell surrounding the hydrophilic head groups, which facilitates the migration and effective adsorption of amphiphilic NCDs at the oil-water interface. However, with a further increase in NaCl concentration (up to 10 wt%), the interfacial tension of samples with shorter alkyl chains began to rise (e.g., for C12-NCDs, the interfacial tension rose from 0.08895 mN/m to 0.15230 mN/m). This increase is likely due to micelle aggregation at high salt concentrations, which impedes the diffusion and arrangement of carbon dots at the interface [39]. Notably, C18-NCDs exhibited exceptional resistance to salt-induced aggregation, maintaining ultra-low interfacial tension even in high-salinity conditions (e.g., 0.00119 mN/m at 10 wt% NaCl). This enhanced stability is attributed to the extended hydrophobic chains, which effectively mitigate aggregation effects and maintain structural integrity at the interface. These findings confirm that increasing the alkyl chain length not only enhances the interfacial activity of the carbon dots but also improves their stability at the oil-water interface under high-salinity conditions. The interfacial tension data presented in Table S5 further highlight the exceptional oil-water interfacial regulation performance of the synthesized amphiphilic NCDs compared to other CDs reported in the literature.

Wettability is a crucial parameter for evaluating the practical applicability of amphiphilic molecules or nanocolloidal materials. To investigate the wetting behavior and dynamic spreading properties of amphiphilic NCDs on hydrophobic surfaces, the contact angles of carbon dot solutions at varying concentrations were measured on paraffin-coated hydrophobic substrates (Fig. 10 ). The results indicated that the contact angles of all sample solutions decreased progressively with increasing concentration, demonstrating a concentration-dependent adsorption behavior at the solid-liquid interface. This trend can be attributed to the intrinsic properties of amphiphilic NCDs. As the solution concentration increased, the driving force for monomer diffusion toward the solid surface intensified, promoting the formation of a dense adsorption layer at the hydrophobic interface, which enhanced droplet spreading and improved wetting performance [58].

Further analysis of dynamic contact angle evolution over time (from 10 s to 100 s) demonstrated that alkyl chain length significantly influenced the wetting kinetics of the carbon dots. C12-NCDs and C14-NCDs exhibited relatively limited improvements in wetting performance. At the highest concentration (1000 mg/L), the contact angles for C12-NCDs and C14-NCDs decreased only slightly from 99.55° and 98.50° at 10 s to 93.69° and 87.36° at 100 s, respectively. This limited reduction suggests that the shorter alkyl chains provide relatively weak surface activity, insufficient to effectively overcome the hydrophobic energy barrier of the paraffin surface within a short period, resulting in only minor wetting enhancement. In contrast, C16-NCDs and C18-NCDs exhibited significantly improved wetting performance under the same conditions, with contact angles decreasing dramatically to 58.18° and 44.63°, respectively. This enhanced performance can be explained by the interfacial adsorption behavior of amphiphilic molecules. On the hydrophobic surface, the longer hydrophobic alkyl chains of the carbon dots interact strongly with the solid surface, forming stable anchoring points. This anchoring effect promotes the formation of a tightly packed and ordered adsorption layer at the interface. The extended alkyl chains reinforce hydrophobic interactions with the paraffin-coated surface, facilitating the formation of a denser and more stable adsorption layer, which significantly improves wetting and spreading performance [6,59].

The pronounced reduction in dynamic contact angle over time further highlights that C16-NCDs and C18-NCDs possess faster interfacial diffusion rates and stronger adsorption capabilities, enabling rapid wetting and spreading of droplets on the solid surface. Consequently, C18-NCDs exhibited the most efficient wetting performance, owing to its longer hydrophobic alkyl chains. This trend is consistent with the observed interfacial activity results, further confirming that the wetting performance of amphiphilic carbon dots is closely linked to the structure of their hydrophobic chains.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance for dispersing and stabilizing MWCNTs
ce:section.ce:para: Due to their unique graphite-layered structure, outstanding physicochemical properties, and high aspect ratio, MWCNTs have garnered considerable attention in materials science and energy-related applications [60–62]. However, the sp2-hybridized carbon atoms on the MWCNT surface create an extensive delocalized π-electron system, resulting in strong van der Waals forces and π-π interactions between nanotubes. Combined with their inherently hydrophobic nature, this characteristic makes MWCNTs highly prone to entanglement and aggregation in aqueous systems, significantly limiting their practical applications [45,63,64]. Consequently, achieving stable and effective MWCNT dispersion remains a key challenge in nanomaterials research.

Fig. 11 presents a comprehensive evaluation of the dispersing and stabilizing performance of amphiphilic NCDs with different alkyl chain lengths for MWCNTs. The assessment involved DLS particle size measurements, Zeta potential analysis, and visual observation of MWCNT suspensions after 72 h of static settling. As shown in Fig. 11a, the particle size distribution and PDI data clearly indicate that C16-NCDs and C18-NCDs exhibited superior dispersing performance compared to C12-NCDs and C14-NCDs. The MWCNT suspension stabilized by C18-NCDs achieved the smallest average particle size of 231 nm, suggesting that the longer hydrophobic alkyl chains facilitated stronger hydrophobic interactions with the MWCNT surface. This enhanced interaction effectively promoted nanotube disentanglement and dispersion while suppressing re-aggregation [65].

The Zeta potential results (Fig. 11b) further confirmed that the absolute Zeta potential values of dispersed MWCNTs increased significantly with alkyl chain length, rising from −21.1 mV for C12-NCDs to −31.9 mV for C18-NCDs. This trend is closely related to the increased surface charge density effect induced by the extended hydrophobic chain length in ionic surfactants. The extended alkyl chains improved the tight adsorption and ordered arrangement of NCD micelles on the MWCNT surface, effectively increasing the distribution density of sulfonate groups. This heightened electrostatic barrier efficiently mitigated van der Waals force-induced aggregation, ensuring better dispersion stability [55,66].

Visual observations of MWCNT dispersions after 72 h of static settling (Fig. 11c) provided further evidence. At all tested concentrations (1–1000 mg/L), MWCNT suspensions dispersed using C12-NCDs and C14-NCDs showed obvious sedimentation, indicating that short-chain amphiphilic NCDs failed to form an effective and stable adsorption layer on the MWCNT surface. This was likely due to insufficient hydrophobic interactions between the short alkyl chains and the MWCNT surface, resulting in slower adsorption rates and weaker binding strength, which were inadequate to provide full surface coverage for the hydrophobic nanotubes [66]. By contrast, MWCNT suspensions dispersed with C16-NCDs (1000 mg/L) and C18-NCDs (≥500 mg/L) remained stably suspended even after 72 h, further confirming that longer-chain amphiphilic NCDs formed a robust and stable adsorption layer on the nanotube surface, effectively preventing re-aggregation caused by van der Waals interactions [67]. Additionally, amphiphilic NCDs possess a rich conjugated aromatic structure, which can engage in strong π-π interactions with the sp2 carbon framework of MWCNTs. This distinctive non-covalent interaction facilitates stronger interfacial anchoring between amphiphilic NCDs and MWCNTs, further enhancing their long-term dispersion stability [68].

In this study, a series of novel amphiphilic NCDs were synthesized from bituminous coal via a multi-step process involving green HCOOH/H2O2-mediated oxidation-depolymerization, hydrothermal nitrogen doping, alkyl chain functionalization, and sulfonation. Their structure, surface/interfacial properties, and performance for dispersing MWCNTs in aqueous solutions were systematically evaluated. The key findings are summarized as follows:
(1)
The synergistic effect of the HCOOH/H2O2 oxidation system in promoting the synthesis of coal-based CDs was elucidated, and the optimal oxidation conditions were identified as a volume ratio of 5:1 and a reaction time of 18 h, yielding 23.0 %. TEM, FT-IR, and XPS analyses confirmed the successful synthesis of carbon dots and their amphiphilic functionalization. UV–Vis and PL spectroscopy revealed that the shielding effect of long-chain alkyl groups suppressed the exposure of localized defects, resulting in reduced fluorescence intensity and a slight redshift in emission.
(2)
Interfacial performance tests revealed that increasing the alkyl chain length from C12 to C18 substantially improved both surface and oil-water interfacial activity. The CMC at 25 °C decreased from 802 mg/L to 121 mg/L, with a corresponding drop in γCMC. Micelle size increased from 69 to 83 nm to 126–142 nm, while the PDI remained below 0.4 and Zeta potential increased from −35.2 mV to −42.4 mV, indicating enhanced colloidal stability. Notably, the oil-water interfacial tension dropped from 0.58866 mN/m (C12-NCDs) to 0.02194 mN/m (C18-NCDs) in the absence of salt, and to an ultra-low 0.00119 mN/m under 10 wt% NaCl, reflecting excellent interfacial film-forming capacity under harsh conditions.
(3)
Contact angle measurements further confirmed the superior wettability of C18-NCDs, which exhibited the fastest spreading dynamics and the lowest contact angle (44.63°) on paraffin-coated surfaces, driven by strong hydrophobic interactions. Moreover, C18-NCDs showed excellent dispersibility for MWCNTs, attributed to a synergistic mechanism involving π–π stacking with the nanotube surface, hydrophobic anchoring via extended alkyl chains, and electrostatic repulsion provided by sulfonate groups.

This study not only clarifies the structure–property relationships of coal-based amphiphilic carbon dots, but also demonstrates their strong application potential as high-performance interfacial regulators and nanodispersants in areas such as petroleum recovery, fuel emulsification, and nanomaterial engineering. To facilitate wider industrial implementation, we recommend that policymakers support the development of low-emission, high-value coal utilization technologies. This could be achieved by increasing funding for green chemical transformation research, integrating coal-derived nanomaterial innovation into national strategies for energy transition and advanced manufacturing, and promoting collaborative scientific programs that combine coal chemistry, materials science, and environmental technology to expand the high-end utilization of coal resources.

Despite the promising findings, this study has certain limitations. Due to the ultrasmall size and structural heterogeneity of carbon dots, it remains challenging to precisely quantify the grafting density of hydrophilic and hydrophobic groups on individual particles. In addition, the role of mineral matter present in the raw coal and the influence of precursor feed ratios (e.g., 1-chloroalkanes and sulfamic acid) on surface functionalization and interfacial performance have not yet been fully investigated. Future studies will focus on refining functionalization strategies, examining the effect of reaction sequence on structural and functional properties, and conducting comparative evaluations across different coal sources and process parameters to provide a more comprehensive foundation for practical application.
